questions.prompt: A 4-agent prototype costs $0.40/run and 35s latency. A single agent matches quality at $0.10/run and 12s. Ship which?

choices.text: Single agent. Multi-agent was complexity without value. Reserve multi-agent for cases where roles cleanly differ (different tools/models/safety profiles), tool overload forces a split (15+), parallel workstreams, or different approval requirements.

questions.explanation: Default single-agent. Promote to multi-agent when single is genuinely cracking — same quality cheaper means the multi-agent design wasn't justified.

questions.prompt: You need an agent to plan, run several sub-research queries, and synthesize. Which topology fits?

choices.text: Supervisor — a manager agent orchestrates specialists as tools. The supervisor plans, calls sub-research specialists, composes the synthesis. Fits decompose-then-synthesize work where one brain coordinates multiple specialists.

questions.explanation: Supervisor for decompose + synthesize. Routing for clear category separation. Hand-off for conversational personas. Pick by task shape.

questions.prompt: What's the production short-term memory pattern for long conversations?

choices.text: Sliding window (last N verbatim) + rolling summary of older turns + structured task state for critical facts (order IDs, decisions, user preferences). Stack with prompt caching for major cost reduction.

questions.explanation: Three layers: verbatim recent for continuity, summarized older for gist, structured state for critical facts that must persist verbatim. Cache the stable summary + system prompt for ~90% off the cached input portion.

questions.prompt: An agent infers 'user likes Python' from one ambiguous message. Two months later, every session is Python-flavored. User actually prefers Go. Fix?

choices.text: Tag every stored fact with confidence + source. Auto-expire low-confidence inferences after a window. Add a user-facing 'review my memory' UI so users can correct stored facts. The original problem: treating an inference as canonical.

questions.explanation: Memory poisoning is preventable with a trust hierarchy: explicit > confirmed > inferred > behavioral. Higher trust persists; lower trust expires. User review keeps memory honest.

questions.prompt: What MUST you eval in a long-term memory system?

choices.text: Recall + boundedness (no irrelevant past context) + update (current values used when facts change) + cross-user isolation (no leaks). The last is critical — cross-user memory leaks are major privacy incidents.

questions.explanation: Memory has multiple failure modes. Eval each. Cross-user leaks are especially nasty — bake the test into your CI.
